chunk_summary: This section outlines the outpatient pathway for confirming and managing IgE-mediated food allergies after anaphylaxis, emphasizing structured follow-up, diagnostic methods, and safety planning for home and school settings.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of structured outpatient pathways after anaphylaxis to prevent recurrence and improve safety.
Learn diagnostic approaches for IgE-mediated food allergies, including allergy-focused history, targeted testing, and supervised oral food challenges (OFC).
Recognize the components of a home-and-school safety bundle for confirmed food allergies.
Explore proactive follow-up strategies to reassess accidental exposures and refresh emergency training.
key_facts: Post-anaphylaxis follow-up should occur within 1–2 weeks to review diagnosis and action plans, and at 3–6 months to reassess exposures and training.

Indian guidelines recommend using skin prick tests (SPT) or food-specific IgE testing for diagnosis, with supervised oral food challenges (OFC) as the gold standard when history and tests are discordant.
A positive allergy test without symptoms indicates sensitization, not allergy.
Every confirmed IgE-mediated food allergy requires a safety bundle: avoidance counseling, emergency action plan, and access to intramuscular epinephrine with training.
School safety planning should include a written note specifying allergens, symptom recognition, and epinephrine storage.
reference_quote: Indian guidance emphasizes that a convincing immediate reaction history plus a positive SPT (or food-specific sIgE when SPT is not feasible) supports diagnosis, while a positive test without symptoms indicates sensitization.
document_key_topics: Diagnostic methods for IgE-mediated food allergies
Safety planning for food allergy in home and school settings
Proactive follow-up strategies for allergy care
